description: A surface is 'keloa' when its grain or other irregularities catch the hand or resist easy passage. The adjective extends to abrasive delivery in 'keloa haoni' (rough voice) and 'keloa haolu' (rough speaking). That extension concerns manner: loudness is 'theisa' (loud), while constrained choice is named by 'kawhera' (coerce).
articulatory_notes: The back of the tongue closes at the soft palate for 'k' and releases into true-mid front 'e'. The tongue tip then meets the alveolar ridge for 'l' before rounded mid-back 'o'. Stress falls on 'lo', and final open central 'a' follows in a distinct hiatus syllable.
usage_notes: The phrase 'keloa kerou' means rough stone. Its contrast with 'helu' (smooth) reports texture or passage, not workmanship or suitability.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: The North Wind transmutation places 'keloa haolu' (rough speaking) beside 'kawhera' (coerce). Abrasive manner and constrained choice remain separate facts.

semantic_domains.dialogue: an abrasive texture extended to the delivery of a voice or utterance
semantic_domains.physical: an uneven or coarse surface that catches against touch or motion
